0.text: DIFC Courts Rules of Court Order No. 2 of 2017 Amending Part 44 of the Rules of the DIFC Courts

2.text: of Order No. 2 DIFC Courts (/glossary/difc-courts/) Rules (/glossary/rules/) Court (/glossary/court/) of 2017 Amending Part 44 of the Rules of the DIFC Courts

3.text: I, Michael Hwang, of the DIFC Courts, make the following Chief Justice (/glossary/chief-justice/) Order:

4.text: In exercise of the powers conferred on me by Article 8(3)(a) of Dubai Law No. 9 of 2004, as amended; and after having reviewed:

5.text:
Dubai Law No. 9 of 2004 in respect of the , as amended;DIFC (/glossary/difc/) Dubai Law No. 12 of 2004 in respect of the Judicial Authority at the DIFC, as amended; DIFC Law No. 10 of 2004 in respect of the DIFC Courts Law;   
and

6.text: DIFC Order No. 1 of 2014 in respect of the Rules of the DIFC Courts

7.text:
1. This Order may be cited as the Rules of Court Order No. 2 of 2017.   
2. Part 44 of the Rules of the DIFC Courts (RDC) on Appeals shall be amended in accordance with Schedule 1 to this Order.   
3. This shall first be reflected in the online version of the RDC, and subsequently in future hardcopy publications.   
4. The amended Rules will come into force on 4 April 2017.

8.text: Michael Hwang Chief Justice of the DIFC Courts

11.text: Scope of this part and interpretation

13.text: The Rules in this Part apply to appeals to:

14.text: (1) the Court of Appeal; and (2) the Court of First Instance.

17.text: (1) ‘appeal Court’ means the Court to which an appeal is made;G

18.text: (2) ‘lower Court’ means the Court , or other person or body fromG tribunal (/glossary/tribunal/)G whose decision an appeal is brought;

19.text: (3) unless the use or context otherwise requires, ‘decision’ means an order or direction or, where a matter is to be disposed of otherwise than by order or direction, the pronouncement of the disposal;

20.text: (4) ‘appellant’ means a person who brings or seeks to bring an appeal;

22.text: (a) a person other than the appellant who was a party to the proceedings in the lower Court andG G who is affected by the appeal; and

23.text: (b) a person who is permitted by the appeal Court to be a party to the appeal; andG

24.text: (6) ‘appeal notice’ means an appellant’s or respondent’s notice.G G

26.text: This Part is subject to any Rule, enactment or Practice Direction which sets out special provisions with regard to any particular category of appeal.

29.text: Unless the appeal Court or the lower Court orders otherwise, an appeal shall not operate as a G G of any decision of the lower Court .stay (/glossary/stay/)G G

32.text: Schedule 1 to DIFC Courts Rules of Court Order No. 2 of 2017

33.text: An appellant or respondent requires permission to appeal:G G

34.text:
(1) where the appeal is to the Court of Appeal, except where the appeal is against a committal order; (2) where the appeal is to the Court of First Instance, except where the appeal is against:   
(a) a refusal by the of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer Registrar (/glossary/registrar/) incorporation under Article 122(1) of the Companies Law (Amended and Restated), DIFC Law 3 of 2006; or   
(b) a refusal by the Registrar of Companies to grant authorisation to transfer a Limited Partnership under Article 63(1) of the Limited Partnership Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2006.

37.text: An appellant’s application for permission to appeal must be made to the lower Court:

38.text: (1) orally at the hearing at which the decision to be appealed was made; or (2) in an appellant’s notice.

40.text: Where an oral application as referred to in Rule 44.6(1) above has not been made, but a party requests further time to make such an application, the lower Court may adjourn the hearing to give that party the opportunity to do so.

42.text: The lower Court may refer an application for permission to appeal to the appeal Court for decision.

44.text: Where the lower Court refuses to give permission to appeal, a further application for permission to appeal may be made to the appeal Court in an appellant’s notice.

47.text: The appellant must fi le the appellant’s notice as referred to in Rule 44.6(2):

48.text: (1) within such period as may be directed by the lower Court; or

49.text: (2) where the lower Court makes no such direction, within 21 days after the date of the decision.

51.text: The appellant must fi le the appellant’s notice as referred to in Rule 44.9:

52.text: (1) within such period as may be directed by the lower Court; or

53.text: (2) where the lower Court makes no such direction; within 21 days after receipt of the notifi cation of the decision refusing permission to appeal

55.text: The parties may not agree to extend the time for appeal.

57.text: Where the time for appeal has expired the appellant must fi le the appellant’s notice and include therein –

58.text: (1) an application for an extension of time; and

59.text: (2) a statement of the reason for the delay and the steps taken prior to the application being made.

62.text: (1) A respondent may make written submissions in opposition to an application for permission to appeal.

63.text: (2) A respondent wishing to make submissions in opposition to permission to appeal must fi le and serve the submissions within 21 days of the upon him of the appellant’sservice (/glossary/service/) notice (see RDC 44.34).

66.text: An application for permission to appeal referred to the appeal Court by the lower Court or made to the appeal Court in an appellant’s notice may not be decided by the againstJudge (/glossary/judge/) whose decision permission to appeal is sought.

68.text: An application for permission to appeal not made orally to the lower Court at the hearing will ordinarily be decided without an oral hearing.

70.text: The appellant may request that the application for permission to appeal be considered at an oral hearing, supported by grounds as to why it would be in the interests of justice to do so.

72.text: The lower Court or the appeal Court may direct:

73.text: (1) the of further submissions by the appellant or any respondent;filing (/glossary/filing/) (2) that the application for permission to appeal be considered at an oral hearing.

75.text: Permission to appeal may only be given where the lower Court or the appeal Court considers that:

76.text: (1) the appeal would have a real prospect of success; or (2) there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard.

78.text: If permission to appeal is granted without an oral hearing, the parties will be notifi ed of that decision.

80.text: If permission to appeal is refused without an oral hearing, the parties will be notifi ed of that decision and brief reasons for it.

83.text: An order giving permission to appeal may:

84.text: (1) limit the issues to be heard; and (2) be made subject to conditions.

86.text: Where the lower Court or the appeal Court gives permission to appeal on some issues only, it will:G

87.text:
(1) refuse permission to appeal on any remaining issues; or   
(2) reserve the question of permission to appeal on any remaining issues to the Court hearing theG appeal.

89.text: If the lower Court reserves the question of permission to appeal underG Rule 44.23 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\therefore d = 7 7 5 2 )$ (2),the appellant must, within 14 days after receipt of the notifi cation of the decision, inform theG appeal Court and the respondent in writing whether he intends to pursue the reserved issues. If theG G appellant does intend to pursue the reserved issues, the parties must include in any time estimate forG the appeal hearing, their time estimate for the reserved issues.

92.text: The lower Court or the appeal Court will normally allow the respondent his costs of an application G G for permission to appeal if permission to appeal is refused.

95.text: Case management decisions include decisions made under

96.text: Rule 4.2 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\mathrm { i d } = 5 0 1 6 ) .$ ) and decisions about:

99.text: Where the application is for permission to appeal from a case management decision, the lower CourtG or the appeal Court deciding the application may take into account whether:

100.text: (1) the issue is of suffi cient signifi cance to justify the costs of an appeal;

101.text: (2) the procedural consequences of an appeal (e.g. loss of trial date) outweigh the signifi cance of the case management decision; and

102.text: (3) it would be more convenient to determine the issue at or after trial.

105.text: An appellant’s notice must be fi led in all cases other than an application for permission to appeal made orally as referred to in Rule 44.6(1).

107.text: Subject to Rule 44.30, an appellant’s notice must:

108.text: (1) set out the grounds of appeal relied on and (2) include or be accompanied by a skeleton argument.

110.text: Where it is impracticable to comply with Rule 44.29, a statement of the grounds of appeal and the skeleton argument must be fi led within 21 days of fi ling the appellant’s notice.

113.text: (1) set out clearly the reasons why it is said the decision of the lower Court was:G

115.text: (b) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower Court ;G

116.text: (2) specify, in respect of each ground, whether the ground raises an appeal on a point of law or is an appeal against a fi nding of fact; and

117.text: (3) state the orders sought on appeal.

119.text: An appellant’s notice which includes a skeleton argument or a skeleton argument fi led in compliance with Rule 44.30 must be accompanied by –

120.text: (1) where an application for permission to appeal is made in an appellant’s notice fi led pursuant to Rule 44.6(2), any witness statement and in support of the application;affidavit (/glossary/affidavit/)

121.text: (2) where an application for permission to appeal is made in an appellant’s notice fi led pursuant to Rule 44.9 –

122.text: (a) a copy of the order refusing permission to appeal, together with a copy of the Judge’s reasons for refusing permission to appeal;

123.text: (b) the claim form and statements of case (where relevant to the subject of the appeal);

124.text: (c) a suitable record of the judgment;

125.text: (d) a copy or record of the decision appealed from;

126.text: (e) those parts of the documentary evidence and any transcript of evidence reasonable considered necessary to enable the appeal Court to decide whether or not to grant permission to appeal; and

127.text: (f) any witness statement and affi davit in support of the application.

129.text: Where the lower Court refers an application for permission to appeal to the appeal Court for decision, the appellant must fi le the documents referred to in Rule 44.32(2) within 14 days of receipt of notifi cation of the referral.

131.text: Unless the Court otherwise orders, the appellant must serve on each respondent –

132.text: (a) a sealed copy of the appellant’s notice;

133.text: (b) copies of any statement of the grounds of appeal and any skeleton argument; and

134.text: (c) copies of the documents accompanying the appellant’s notice or the skeleton argument as referred to in Rule 44.32 as soon as practicable and in any event within 7 days after they are fi led.

136.text: The appellant must provide two additional copies of the documents referred to in Rule 44.32(2) or Rule 44.33 for the appeal Court.

138.text: The appellant must, as soon as practicable, fi le a certifi cate of service of the documents referred toG G in Rule 44.34.

140.text: The Court may dispense with the requirement for service of the appellant’s notice on a respondentG G G

141.text: Any application noticeG seeking an order under

142.text: Rule 9.32 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\dot { 1 } 0 1 = 5 2 7 4$ ) to dispense with service should set out the reasons relied on and be verifi ed by a statement of truth.G

145.text: An application to be made to the appeal Court for a remedy incidental to the appeal may be included G in the appellant’s notice G or made in a

148.text: Where an application for a remedy incidental to the appeal is included in the appellant’s notice, the relating to Part 23 application notices apply mutatis mutandis to the rules (/glossary/rules/) application.

151.text: (1) Where permission to appeal is not required or has been given, the appellant must fi le an appeal bundle not less than 21 days before the appeal hearing and serve a copy of the appeal bundle on each respondent at the same time as he fi les it.

152.text: (2) The appellant must provide two additional copies of the appeal bundle for the appeal Court.

154.text: (a) a sealed copy of the appellant’s notice;G

155.text: (b) a sealed copy of the decision appealed from;

156.text: (c) a copy of any order giving or refusing permission to appeal, together with a copy of the Judge’s G reasons for allowing or refusing permission to appeal;

157.text: (d) any affi davit or witness statement fi led in support of any application included in the appellant’s G G notice;

158.text: (e) a copy of his skeleton argument;

159.text: (f) the claim form and statements of case (where relevant to the subject of the appeal);

160.text: (g) a suitable record of the judgment, and those parts of any transcript of evidence which are directly relevant to any question at issue on the appeal;

161.text: (h) those parts of any documentary evidence and any transcript of evidence reasonably considered necessary to enable the appeal Court to decide the appeal;

162.text: (i) any application notice (or case management documentation) relevant to the subject of the appeal;G

163.text: (j) in the case of judicial review or a statutory appeal, the original decision which was the subject of the application to the lower Court ;G

164.text: (k) in cases where the appeal is from a tribunal , a copy of the tribunal’s reasons for the decision, a G G copy of the decision reviewed by the tribunal and the reasons for the original decision and any G document fi led with the tribunal setting out the grounds of appeal from that decision;G

165.text: (l) any other documents which the appellant reasonably considers necessary to enable the appealG Court to decide the appeal and any application; andG

166.text: (m) such other documents as the Court may direct.G

168.text: All documents that are extraneous to the issues to be considered must be excluded. The appeal bundle may include affi davits , witness statements, summaries, experts’ reports and exhibits but only G where these are directly relevant to the subject matter of the appeal.

170.text: The appeal bundle must contain a certifi cate signed by the appellant’s legal representatives to the G G effect that they have read and understood Rules 44.40 and 44.41 and that the composition of the appeal bundle complies with it.

172.text: Where it is not possible to fi le all the above documents, the appellant must indicate which documents G have not yet been fi led and the reasons why they are not currently available. The appellant mustG then provide a reasonable estimate of when the missing document or documents can be fi led and fi le them as soon as reasonably practicable.

175.text: In cases where the appeal bundle comprises more than 500 pages, exclusive of transcripts, the appellant’s legal representatives must, after consultation with the respondent’s legalG G G representatives , also prepare and fi le with the Court , in addition to copies of the appeal bundle (as G G amended in accordance with

176.text: Rule 44.91 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7884) ) the requisite number of copies of a core bundle.

178.text: The core bundle must be fi led within 28 days of receipt of the order giving permission to appeal or, where permission to appeal was granted by the lower Court or is not required, within 28 days of theG date of service of the appellant’s notice on the respondent .G G G

181.text: (1) must contain the documents which are central to the appeal; and

185.text: Rules 44.4 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\mathbf { \dot { l } d = } 7 8 0 1$ ) 8 to 44.5 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7823)9 apply to the preparation of appeal bundles, supplemental respondents’ bundles where the parties are G unable to agree amendments to the appeal bundle, and core bundles.

188.text: Where documents are copied unnecessarily or bundled incompletely, costs may be disallowed. Where the provisions of this Part as to the preparation or delivery of bundles are not followed the bundle may be rejected by the Court or be made the subject of a special costs order.G

191.text: No more than one copy of any document should be included unless there is a good reason for doing otherwise (such as the use of a separate core bundle in Rule 44.46 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle \mathbf { i d } = 7 7 9 5 )$ ).

194.text: The following rules regarding pagination shall apply:

195.text: (1) Bundles must be paginated, each page being numbered individually and consecutively. The pagination used at trial must also be indicated. Letters and other documents should normally be included in chronological order. (An exception to consecutive page numbering arises in the case of core bundles where it may be preferable to retain the original numbering).

196.text: (2) Page numbers should be inserted in bold fi gures at the bottom of the page and in a form that can be clearly distinguished from any other pagination on the document.

199.text: The following rules regarding format and presentation shall apply:

200.text: (1) Where possible the documents should be in A4 format and may be printed single or double sided (unless the Court directs a particular format). Where a document has to be read across rather than down the page, it should be so placed in the bundle as to ensure that the text starts nearest the spine.

201.text: (2) Where any marking or writing in colour on a document is important, the document must be copied in colour or marked up correctly in colour.

202.text: (3) Documents which are not easily legible should be transcribed and the transcription marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed.

203.text: (4) Documents in a language other than English should be translated and the translation marked and placed adjacent to the document translated. The translation should be agreed or, if it cannot be agreed, each party’s proposed translation should be included.

204.text: (5) The size of any bundle should be tailored to its contents. A large lever arch fi le should not be used for just a few pages nor should fi les be overloaded.

205.text: (6) Where it will assist the Court , different sections of the fi le may be separated by cardboard orG other tabbed dividers so long as these are clearly indexed. Where, for example, a document is awaited when the appeal bundle is fi led, a single sheet of paper can be inserted after a divider, indicating the nature of the document awaited. For example, ‘Transcript of evidence of Mr J Smith (to follow)’.

208.text: The following rules regarding binding shall apply:

209.text: (1) All documents, with the exception of transcripts, must be bound together. This may be in a lever arch fi le, ring binder or plastic folder. Plastic sleeves containing loose documents must not be used. Binders and fi les must be strong enough to withstand heavy use.

210.text: (2) Large documents such as plans should be placed in an easily accessible fi le. Large documents which will need to be opened up frequently should be inserted in a fi le larger than A4 size.

213.text: The following rules regarding indices and labels shall apply:

214.text: (1) An index must be included at the front of the bundle listing all the documents and providing the page references for each. In the case of documents such as letters, invoices or bank statements, they may be given a general description.

215.text: (2) Where the bundles consist of more than one fi le, an index to all the fi les should be included in the fi rst fi le and an index included for each fi le. Indices should, if possible, be on a single sheet. The full name of the case should not be inserted on the index if this would waste space. Documents should be identifi ed briefl y but properly.

218.text: The following rules regarding identifi cation shall apply:

219.text: (1) Every bundle must be clearly identifi ed, on the spine and on the front cover, with the name of the case and the Court’s reference. Where the bundle consists of more than one fi le, each fi le must beG numbered on the spine, the front cover and the inside of the front cover.

220.text: (2) Outer labels should use large lettering e.g. ‘Appeal Bundle A’ or ‘Core Bundle’. The full title of the appeal and legal representatives’ names and addresses should be omitted. A label should be used G on the front as well as on the spine.

223.text: All staples, heavy metal clips, etc., must be removed.

226.text: The following rules regarding statements of case shall apply:

227.text: (1) Statements of case should be assembled in ‘chapter’ form — i.e. claim followed by particulars of claim, followed by further information, irrespective of date.

228.text: (2) Redundant documents, e.g. particulars of claim overtaken by amendments, requests for further information recited in the answers given, should generally be excluded.

231.text: The following rules regarding new documents shall apply:

232.text: (1) Before a new document is introduced into bundles which have already been delivered to the Court , steps should be taken to ensure that it carries an appropriate bundle/page number so that itG can be added to the Court documents. It should not be stapled and it should be prepared with punchG holes for immediate inclusion in the binders in use.

233.text: (2) If it is expected that a large number of miscellaneous new documents will from time to time be introduced, there should be a special tabbed empty loose-leaf fi le for that purpose. An index should be produced for this fi le, updated as necessary.

236.text: Since correspondence between legal representatives is unlikely to be required for the purposes of anG appeal, only those letters which will need to be referred to should be copied.

239.text: If the appellant fails to comply with the requirements as to the provision of bundles of documents, the G application or appeal will be referred for consideration to be given as to why it should not be dismissed for failure so to comply.

242.text: A skeleton argument fi led on behalf of the appellant should contain in paragraph 1 the legalG representatives’ time estimate for the hearing of the appeal.G

244.text: A skeleton argument must contain a numbered list of the points which the party wishes to make. These should both defi ne and confi ne the areas of controversy. Each point should be stated as concisely as the nature of the case allows.

246.text: Skeleton arguments submitted in the context of appeals and supplementary skeleton arguments for appeals are not to exceed 35 pages in length. Requests for permission to submit lengthier skeleton arguments should be made via letter application.

248.text: A numbered point must be followed by a reference to any document on which the party wishes to rely.

250.text: A skeleton argument must state, in respect of each authority cited:

251.text: (1) the proposition of law that the authority demonstrates; and (2) the parts of the authority (identifi ed by page or paragraph references) that support the proposition.

253.text: If more than one authority is cited in support of a given proposition, the skeleton argument must briefl y state the reason for taking that course.

256.text: Rule 44.6 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7832)5 should not materially add to the length of the skeleton argument but should be suffi cient to demonstrate, in the context of the argument:

257.text: (1) the relevance of the authority or authorities to that argument; and

258.text: (2) that the citation is necessary for a proper presentation of that argument.

260.text: The cost of preparing a skeleton argument which:

261.text: (1) does not comply with the requirements set out in Rules

264.text: (2) was not fi led within the time limits provided by this Part (or any further time granted by the Court); will not be allowed on assessment except to the extent that the Court otherwise directs.G

266.text: The appellant should consider what other information the appeal Court will need. This may includeG G a list of persons who feature in the case or glossaries of technical terms. A chronology of relevant events will be necessary in most appeals.

269.text: A suitable record of the judgment is –

270.text: (1) where the Court has issued the judgment sealed by the Court, a copy of the judgment; (2) In other cases of Court judgments –

271.text: (a) where the judgment has been offi cially recorded by the Court, a transcript of that record certifi ed by the appellant’s as a correct record;legal representative (/glossary/legal-representative/)

272.text: (b) subject to Rule 44.69(3), where the judgment has not been offi cially recorded by the Court, a note of the judgment agreed between the legal representatives of the appellant and the respondent approved by the Judge whose decision is being appealed.

273.text: (3) If the parties cannot agree on a single note of the judgment, both versions should be provided to the with an explanatory letter. For the purposes of an application forjudge (/glossary/judge/) permission to appeal the note need not be agreed by the respondent or approved by the Judge.

274.text: (4) In tribunal cases, a sealed copy of the tribunal’s reasons for the decision.

276.text: An appellant may not be able to obtain a suitable record of the lower Court’s judgment within the G G time within which the appellant’s notice must be fi led. In such cases the appellant’s notice must stillG G be completed to the best of the appellant’s ability on the basis of the documentation available.G However it may be amended subsequently with the permission of the appeal Court .G

279.text: When the evidence is relevant to the appeal an offi cial transcript of the relevant evidence must be obtained. Transcripts or notes of evidence are generally not needed for the purpose of determining an application for permission to appeal.

281.text: If evidence relevant to the appeal was not offi cially recorded, a typed version of the Judge’s notes ofG evidence must be obtained.

282.text: Procedure after permission to appeal is given

285.text:
44.74 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $=$ 2725&element_id=7851) to   
44.75 set out the procedure where permission to appeal is given.

287.text: The appeal Court will send the parties G

288.text: (1) notifi cation of the date of the hearing; (2) a copy of the order giving permission to appeal; and (3) any other directions given by the Court .G

291.text: The legal representatives who will argue the appeal must provide a time estimate within 14 days ofG the appellant receiving the order giving permission to appeal. It should exclude the time required by G the Court to give judgment. If the respondent disagrees with the time estimate, the respondentG G G must inform the Court within 7 days of receipt of the estimate. In the absence of such notifi cation the G respondent will be deemed to have accepted the estimate proposed on behalf of the appellant .G G

294.text: A respondent may fi le and serve a respondent’s notice – G G

295.text: (1) applying for permission to appeal; or

296.text: (2) asking the appeal Court to uphold the decision of the lower Court for reasons different from orG G additional to those given by the lower Court .G

298.text: (1) A respondent who wishes to ask the appeal Court to vary the decision of the lower Court in anyG G G way must appeal and permission will be required on the same basis as for an appellant .G

299.text: (2) A respondent’s application for permission to appeal must be made to the appeal Court.

300.text: (3) The rules in relation to applying for permission to appeal in an appellant’s notice and decision of the application apply mutatis mutandis to an application for permission to appeal in a respondent’s notice.

302.text: A respondent who wishes only to request that the appeal Court upholds the decision of the lowerG G Court for reasons different from or additional to those given by the lower Court does not appeal andG G does not require permission to appeal.

304.text: If the respondent does not fi le a respondent’s notice, he will not be entitled, except with the G G permission of the Court , to rely on any reason not relied on in the lower Court . G G

307.text: A respondent’s notice must be fi led within:G

308.text: (1) such period as may be directed by the  Court ; orG

309.text: (2) where the Court makes no such direction, 14 days after the date in Rule 44.81.G

313.text: (1) where permission to appeal is not required, the date the respondent is served with the appellant’s notice;

315.text: (a) the date the respondent receives a copy of the order giving permission to appeal; or

316.text: (b) the date the respondent receives notifi cation that the application for permission to appeal and the appeal itself are to be heard together.

318.text: Rule 44.13 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\dot { 1 } 0 = 7 7 7 4$ ) (extension for time for appeal) applies mutatis mutandis to a respondent and a respondent’s notice.G G Accordingly, where an extension of time is required the extension must be requested in the respondent’s notice and the reasons why the respondent failed to act within the specifi ed time mustG G be included.

320.text: The respondent must fi le a skeleton argument in all cases where he proposes to address arguments G to the Court . The respondent’s skeleton argument may be included within a respondent’s notice.G G G

322.text: Unless the Court orders otherwise a respondent’s notice must be served on the appellant and any G G G other respondent :G

323.text: (1) as soon as practicable; and (2) in any event not later than 7 days; after it is fi led.

326.text: (1) fi les a respondent’s notice; butG (2) does not include his skeleton argument within that notice;

327.text: may fi le a skeleton argument accompanying the notice but in any event must fi le his skeleton argument within 14 days of fi ling the notice.G

329.text: A respondent who does not fi le a respondent’s notice but who fi les a skeleton argument must fi le G G that skeleton argument no later than 28 days after the appellant’s skeleton has been fi led.

331.text: The respondent must:G (1) serve his skeleton argument on:

333.text: (b) any other respondent ;G at the same time as he fi les it at the Court ; andG (2) fi le a certifi cate of service .G

336.text: A respondent’s skeleton argument must conform to the directions at Rules G 44. (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7828)60 to 44.6 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle 1 0 = 7 8 3 5 ) 8$ with any necessary modifi cations. It should, where appropriate, answer the arguments set out in the appellant’s skeleton argument.G

339.text: A respondent may include an application within a respondent’s notice in accordance withG G Rule 44.3 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id $\mid =$ 7786)8 above.

342.text: The respondent provide two additional copies of the respondent’s notice for the appeal Court .G G G

344.text: If the respondent wishes to rely on any documents which he reasonably considers necessary to G enable the appeal Court to decide the appeal and any application in addition to those fi led by theG appellant , he must make every effort to agree amendments to the appeal bundle with the appellantG G

346.text: If the legal representatives of the parties are unable to reach agreement, the respondent may G G prepare a supplemental bundle.

348.text: If the respondent prepares a supplemental bundle he must fi le it within 14 days after he is served G with the appeal bundle, and must provide two additional copies for the appeal Court.

350.text: The respondent must serve any supplemental bundle on the appellant and any other respondent at the same time as he fi les it.

353.text: An appeal notice may not be amended without the permission of the appeal Court . An application toG G amend and any application in opposition will normally be dealt with at the appeal hearing unless that course would cause unnecessary expense or delay in which case a request should be made for the application to amend to be heard in advance.

354.text: Striking out appeal notices and setting aside or imposing conditions on permission to appeal

357.text: (1) the whole or part of an appeal notice ; strike out (/glossary/strike-out/)G G (2) permission to appeal in whole or in part;set aside (/glossary/set-aside/)G (3) impose or vary conditions upon which an appeal may be brought.

359.text: The Court G will only exercise its powers under

360.text: Rule 44. (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7844)96 where there is a compelling reason for doing so.

362.text: Where a party was present at an oral hearing at which permission to appeal was given he may not subsequently apply for an order that the Court exercise its powers underG Rule 44.96 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle 1 d = 7 8 4 6 )$

366.text: Once the parties have been notifi ed of the date fi xed for the hearing, the appellant’s legalG representative must, after consultation with his opponent, fi le a bundle containing photocopies of theG authorities upon which each side will rely at the hearing.

368.text: The bundle of authorities should, in general:

371.text: The bundle of authorities must be fi led:

372.text: (1) at least 7 days before the hearing; or (2) where the period of notice of the hearing is less than 7 days, immediately.

374.text: If, through some oversight, a party intends, during the hearing, to refer to other authorities the parties may agree a second agreed bundle. The appellant’s legal representatives must fi le this bundle atG G least 48 hours before the hearing commences.

376.text: A bundle of authorities must bear a certifi cation by the legal representatives responsible for arguing G the case that the requirements of

377.text: Rule 44. (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7831)64 to 44.66 have been complied with in respect of each authority included.

380.text: A supplementary skeleton argument on which the appellant wishes to rely must be fi led no later thanG 14 days after the respondent’s skeleton has been fi led.

383.text: A supplementary skeleton argument on which the respondent wishes to rely must be fi led 14 days G after the appellant’s supplementary skeleton and at least 14 days before the hearing.

385.text: All supplementary skeleton arguments must comply with the requirements set out in Rules 44. (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7828)60 to 44.6 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle 1 0 = 7 8 3 5 ) 8$ .

387.text: At the hearing the Court may refuse to hear argument from a party not contained in a skeletonG argument fi led within the relevant time limit set out in this paragraph.

390.text: All the documents which are needed for the appeal hearing must be fi led at least 7 days before the hearing.

392.text: Any party who fails to comply with the provisions of

396.text: may be required to attend before the Chief Justice to seek permission to proceed with, or to oppose,G the appeal.

397.text: Appeals to the Court of Appeal

399.text: On hearing an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance, the Court of Appeal may:

400.text: (1) make or give any order that could have been made or given by the Court of First Instance;

401.text: (2) attach terms or conditions to an order it makes;

402.text: (3) annul or set aside a decision;G

403.text: (4) require or prohibit the taking of a specifi c action or of action of a specifi ed class;

404.text: (5) make a declaration of facts; or

405.text: (6) make any other order that the Court of Appeal considers appropriate or just.

406.text: Appeals to the Court of First Instance

408.text: Unless another Rule, Practice Direction or enactment provides otherwise, on hearing an appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

409.text: (1) affi rm, reverse or vary the decision appealed;

410.text: (2) set aside the decision appealed, in whole or in part;G

411.text: (3) make any other order it considers appropriate;

412.text: (4) remit proceedings to the tribunal from which the appeal was brought, subject to any directions the G

413.text: Court of First Instance considers appropriate; or

414.text: (5) make any order or direction that is in the interests of justice.

417.text: Every appeal will be limited to a review of the decision of the lower Court unless:G

418.text: (1) the Court considers that in the circumstances of an individual appeal it would be in the interests ofG justice to hold a re-hearing;

424.text: (3) any other Rule or enactment requires a re-hearing.

426.text: The hearing of an appeal will be a re-hearing (as opposed to a review of the decision of the lower Court ) if the appeal is from the decision of a person or other body and the person or other body:G

427.text: (1) did not hold a hearing to come to that decision; or

428.text: (2) held a hearing to come to that decision, but the procedure adopted did not provide for the consideration of evidence.

430.text: The appeal Court may exercise its powers in relation to the whole or part of an order of the lowerG Court . G

432.text: At the hearing of the appeal a party may not rely on a matter not contained in his appeal notice G unless the appeal Court gives permission.G

435.text: Unless it orders otherwise, the appeal Court will not receive:G

436.text: (1) oral evidence; or (2) evidence which was not before the lower Court .G

443.text: , the Court may receive further evidence, including:G

445.text: (4) evidence given in accordance with Part 29 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\vDash$ 2725&element_ $\dot { \mathsf { I d } } = 6 4 6 4$ ).

447.text: The appeal Court may draw any inference of fact which it considers justifi ed on the evidence.G

450.text: The Court of Appeal will allow an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance where the decision of the lower Court was:G

451.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower Court .G

453.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a tribunal provided for in G , DIFC Law or Rules of Court where the decision was:the Law (/glossary/the-law/)G G

454.text: (1) wrong in relation to a question of law; (2) unjust because of procedural unfairness or a miscarriage of justice; and/or (3) wrong in relation to any other matter provided for in or under DIFC Law.

456.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal not referred to in Rule 44.120 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?

459.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings.

462.text: Part 32 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle \mathbf { i d } = 6 8 9 9 )$ offer or payment into Court has been made must not be disclosed to any Judge of the appealG G Court who is to hear or determine: G

463.text: (1) an application for permission to appeal; or (2) an appeal; until all questions (other than costs) have been determined.

468.text: Part 32 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=6899) offer or payment into Court is relevant to the substance of the appeal.G

473.text: does not prevent disclosure in any application in the appeal proceedings if disclosure of the fact that a Part 32 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\operatorname { i d } \left( 6 8 9 9 \right)$ ) offer or payment into Court has been made is properly relevant to the matter to be decided.G

474.text: Who may exercise the powers of the Court of Appeal

476.text: The Registrar may exercise the of the Court of Appeal with theG jurisdiction (/glossary/jurisdiction/)G consent of the Chief Justice to do all things necessary or convenient for the purpose of assisting theG of the Court of Appeal in the exercise of their powers or duties.Judges (/glossary/judge/)G

480.text: A single Judge may exercise the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal relating to an G G application:

481.text: (1) for permission to appeal to the Court of Appeal (and to grant such permission subject to such conditions as may be ordered by the single judge);

482.text: (2) for an extension or reduction of time for any document to be fi led in an appeal to the Court of Appeal (including a general power to amend the times fi xed by the rules or by order of the , as may be the case);court (/glossary/court/)

483.text: (3) for leave to amend the grounds of an appeal to the Court of Appeal;

485.text: (5) to strike out an appeal in the event that:

486.text: (a) the appellant does not fi le a document within the time ordered and notice has been given by the Court in the order for fi ling that the appeal will be struck out if fi ling is not made strictly on time; or

487.text: (b) the appellant does not attend the hearing of the appeal in person or by his Counsel on the date fi xed for hearing; or

488.text: (6) to strike out an appeal in the event that a skeleton is not submitted.

489.text: (7) The single judge shall also have the power to decide any matter of case management relating to the appeal on behalf of the full bench of the Court of Appeal.

491.text: Decisions of the Registrar or a single Judge G G and under Rules 44.125 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\vDash$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle \mathbf { i d } = 7 9 2 3$ ) or 44.126 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7924) may be made without an oral hearing.

493.text: A party may request any decision of the Registrar or the single Judge under RulesG G 44.125 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\mathbf { i d } = 7 9 2 3$ ) or 44.126 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id $\mid =$ 7924)(3) or (4) made without a hearing to be reviewed by the Court of Appeal at an oral hearing.

500.text: must be fi led within 7 days after the party is served with notice of the decision.

502.text: A single Judge may refer any matter for a decision by a Court consisting of two or more G .judges (/glossary/judge/)G

503.text: Dismissal of applications or appeals by consent

505.text: Where an appellant does not wish to pursue an application or an appeal, he may request the appealG Court for an order that his application or appeal be dismissed. If such a request is granted it willG usually be on the basis that the appellant pays the costs of the application or appeal.G

507.text: If the appellant wishes to have the application or appeal dismissed without costs, his request must be G accompanied by a consent signed by the respondent or his legal representative stating that theG G respondent consents to the dismissal of the application or appeal without costs.G

509.text: Where a settlement has been reached disposing of the application or appeal, the parties may make a joint request to the Court asking that the application or appeal be dismissed by consent. If the G request is granted the application or appeal will be dismissed.

510.text: Allowing unopposed appeals or applications without a hearing

512.text: The appeal Court will not normally make an order allowing an appeal unless satisfi ed that the G decision of the lower Court was wrong, but the appeal Court may set aside or vary the order of the G G G lower Court with consent and without determining the merits of the appeal, if it is satisfi ed that there G are good and suffi cient reasons for doing so. Where the appeal Court is requested by all parties to G allow an application or an appeal the Court may consider the request without a hearing. The requestG should set out the relevant history of the proceedings and the matters relied on as justifying the proposed order and be accompanied by a copy of the proposed order.

513.text: Availability of reserved judgments before hand down

516.text: 44.136 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\mathbf { i d } = 7 9 3 7$ ) and 44.137 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\vDash$ 2725&element_ $\mathbf { i d } = 7 9 3 8$ ) apply where the presiding Judge is satisfi ed that the result of the appeal will attract no special degreeG of confi dentiality or sensitivity.

518.text: A copy of the written judgment may be made available to the parties’ legal representatives by 4 p.m.G on the second working day before judgment is due to be pronounced or such other period as the Court may direct. This can be shown, in confi dence, to the parties but only for the purpose ofG obtaining instructions and on the strict understanding that the judgment, or its effect, is not to be disclosed to any other person. A working day is any day on which the Court Offi ce is open for business.

520.text: The appeal will be listed for judgment and the judgment handed down at the appropriate time.

521.text: Attendance of legal representatives on the handing down of a reserved judgment

523.text: Where any consequential orders are agreed, the parties’ legal representatives need not attend on the handing down of a reserved judgment. Where a legal representative does attend the Court may, if itG considers such attendance unnecessary, disallow the costs of the attendance. If the parties do not indicate that they intend to attend, where the appeal was heard by more than one Judge , the G judgment may be handed down by a single member of the Court .G

526.text: The parties must, in respect of any draft agreed orders fi le 4 copies in the Court Offi ce, no later than 12 noon on the working day before the judgment is handed down.

528.text: A copy of a draft order must bear the case reference, the date the judgment is to be handed down and the name of the presiding Judge .G

531.text: Any proposed correction to the draft judgment should be sent to the Judge who prepared the draftG with a copy to any other party.

534.text: Costs are likely to be assessed by way of immediate assessment at the following hearings:

535.text: (1) contested directions hearings; (2) applications for permission to appeal at which the respondent is present;G

536.text: (3) dismissal list hearings in the Court of Appeal at which the respondent is present;G

537.text: (4) appeals from case management decisions; and

538.text: (5) appeals listed for one day or less.

540.text: Parties attending any of the hearings referred to in Rule 44.142 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?

542.text: should be prepared to deal with the immediate assessment.

543.text: Appeals to the Court of First Instance from the Director of Employment Standards

547.text: 44.154 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_ $\scriptstyle 1 d = 7 9 6 0 )$ ) apply to appeals to the Court of First Instance from a determination, decision, or fi ne of the Director of Employment Standards pursuant to Article 83(1) of the Employment Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2005.

549.text: An appeal referred to in Rule 44.144 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?

551.text: shall be fi led within 30 days of receipt of the determination, decision, or fi ne appealed and in accordance with the requirements of this Part.

553.text: The Director of Employment Standards must be named as a party to an appeal referred to in Rule 44.144 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html? rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id=7950)

555.text: The Court may grant a stay of the decision appealed from until the disposition of the appeal.G G

557.text: The Court may attach conditions to the stay , including requiring a party to deposit as security partG G or all of a monetary order.

559.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall provide the Court with the record that was before him atG the time the determination, order or fi ne was made, including any witness statement and documents considered by him.

561.text: The Court may consider any other relevant evidence, in addition to the record.G

563.text: Before considering the appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

564.text: (1) refer the matter back to the Director of Employment Standards for further investigation; or (2) recommend that an attempt be made to settle the matter.

566.text: The Court may dismiss the appeal without a hearing if satisfi ed that:G

567.text: (1) the appeal is not within the Court’s jurisdiction ; G G (2) the appeal is frivolous or trivial or is not brought in good faith.

569.text: On hearing the appeal the Court may:G

572.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall comply with any directions the Court gives to him.G

575.text: No appeal lies from a decision of the Court of First Instance or the Court of Appeal on an appeal.

578.text: The Court of Appeal or the Court of First Instance will not reopen a fi nal determination of any appeal unless:

579.text: (1) it is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;

580.text: (2) the circumstances are exceptional and make it appropriate to reopen the appeal; and (3) there is no alternative effective remedy.

586.text:
44.161 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?rbid $\mid =$ 2725&element_id $\mid =$ 7969) and   
44.165, “appeal” includes an application for permission to appeal.

588.text: Permission is needed to make an application under Rule 44.156 (http://difccourts.complinet.com/en/display/display.html?

590.text: to reopen a fi nal determination of an appeal even in cases where permission was not needed for the original appeal.

592.text: Permission must be sought from the Court whose decision the applicant wishes to reopen.G

594.text: The application for permission must be made by application notice and supported by written G evidence, verifi ed by a statement of truth.

596.text: A copy of the application for permission must not be served on any other party to the original appeal unless the Court so directs.G

598.text: Where the Court directs that the application for permission is to be served on another party, thatG party may within 14 days of the service on him of the copy of the application fi le and serve a written G statement either supporting or opposing the application.

600.text: The application for permission, and any written statements supporting or opposing it, will be considered on paper by a single Judge.G

602.text: There is no right to an oral hearing of an application for permission unless, exceptionally, the Judge G so directs.

604.text: The Judge will not grant permission without directing the application to be served on the other party G to the original appeal and giving him an opportunity to make representations.

606.text: There is no right of appeal or review from the decision of the Judge on the application for permission,G which is fi nal.
